summary: The Iroquois Confederacy (also called the Haudenosaunee, the Five Nations, or after 1722 the Six Nations) was a political alliance of Native American nations based in upstate New York, uniting the Mohawk, Oneida, Onondaga, Cayuga, and Seneca (later joined by the Tuscarora). Founded by the legendary Great Peacemaker (Deganawidah) with the help of Hiawatha and the "Mother of Nations" Jigonsaseh, it was governed under an oral constitution called the Great Law of Peace. Clues cover its Grand Council government of clan-mother-appointed sachems, its expansionist Beaver Wars against the French and Huron, its Covenant Chain diplomacy with the British, the contested "Iroquois influence thesis" on the U.S. Constitution, the Longhouse Religion of the prophet Handsome Lake, its divided loyalties (mostly pro-British, under Joseph Brant) and subsequent devastation by the Sullivan-Clinton Expedition during the American Revolution, and its prominence as a subject of Lewis Henry Morgan's pioneering anthropology.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: The Confederacy is identified in giveaways as an alliance of Native American tribes based in upstate New York, variously called the Haudenosaunee, the Five Nations, or (after the Tuscarora joined) the Six Nations, uniting the Mohawk, Oneida, Onondaga, Cayuga, and Seneca peoples.

clues.clue: Confederacy/alliance of Native American tribes based in upstate New York, also called the Haudenosaunee, Five Nations, or Six Nations
examples: name this American Indian confederacy based in upstate New York.
name this group of allied tribes in upstate New York.

clues.clue: Member nations include the Mohawk, Oneida, Onondaga, Cayuga, and Seneca (joined later by the Tuscarora as a sixth nation)
examples: name this union of the indigenous Oneida, Tuscarora, Onondaga, Cayuga, Seneca, and Mohawk peoples.
the Mohawk belonged to what confederacy of Native American tribes?

works.name: Founding: the Great Peacemaker, Hiawatha, and Jigonsaseh
works.description: The Confederacy's founding is credited to the semi-mythical Great Peacemaker (Deganawidah/Dekanawidah), assisted by Hiawatha and the "Mother of Nations" Jigonsaseh, who together instituted the Great Law of Peace; the founding is symbolized by the burial of weapons beneath an uprooted pine tree.

clues.clue: Founded by the "Great Peacemaker" (Deganawidah/Dekanawidah), who was assisted by Hiawatha, in instituting the Great Law of Peace
examples: A "Great Peacemaker" and Hiawatha were semi-mythical founders of what northeastern Native American confederacy?
This polity was founded by the Mother of Nations Jigonsaseh and Great Peacemaker Deganawidah, who instituted the Great Law of Peace.

clues.clue: Jigonsaseh (also spelled Jigonhsasee), revered as the "Mother of Nations," helped found the Confederacy and its Great Law of Peace alongside Deganawidah
examples: Jigonsaseh was revered as the "Mother of Nations" by this polity, whose Great Law of Peace was codified by Deganawidah and Hiawatha.
The woman Jigonhsasee helped to found this coalition with Dekanawidah, and these people would later fight the French in the Beaver Wars.

clues.clue: During the founding, Jigonsaseh (the Mother of Nations) was instructed that a structure would have five fires but one mind and law
examples: During the formation of this government, a woman known as "the Mother of Nations" was instructed that in a certain structure there would be five fires, but one mind and law.

clues.clue: The Confederacy's legendary creation involved its leaders uprooting a pine tree and casting all their weapons into the hole
examples: The legendary creation of this polity occurred after its leaders uprooted a pine tree and cast all their weapons into the hole.

clues.clue: Founding was driven in part by fear of the neighboring Algonquians
examples: Its foundation was driven in part by fear of the neighboring Algonquians.

clues.clue: The Great Peacemaker, the Confederacy's first leader, reportedly suffered from a severe speech impediment
examples: The first leader of this group suffered from a severe speech impediment, and one member of this group, Ely Parker, wrote the instrument of surrender at Appomattox.

works.name: The Great Law of Peace and Government Structure
works.description: The Confederacy was governed by the Great Law of Peace (Gayanashagowa), an oral constitution codified by Deganawidah and Hiawatha and recorded on wampum belts, which established a Grand Council of representatives symbolized by an eastern white pine, gave clan mothers power over chiefly succession, and designated the centrally located Onondaga as "Keepers of the Fire."

clues.clue: Great Law of Peace: an oral constitution implemented/codified by Dekanawidah and Hiawatha, recorded on wampum belts
examples: These people's oral constitution, the Great Law of Peace, was implemented by Dekanawidah and Hiawatha.
Jigonhasee helped create this government's Great Law of Peace, which was recorded on wampum belts.

clues.clue: Amending the Great Law of Peace was called "adding to the rafters"
examples: The act of amending this document was called "adding to the rafters".

clues.clue: The Great Law of Peace gives women the right to confirm or deny inheritance of political offices, symbolized by their control over strings of shells (wampum)
examples: This document gives women the right to confirm or deny the inheritance of certain political offices, symbolized with their control over strings of shells.

clues.clue: The Great Law of Peace permits granting an advisory office to worthy non-hereditary leaders, who are likened to newly-sprung pine trees
examples: This document permits the occasional granting of an advisory office to worthy people interested in governance; such people were likened to newly-sprung pine trees.

clues.clue: Four joined white squares with a white pine in the middle, originally depicted on a wampum belt, represent the unity codified by the Great Law of Peace
examples: Four joined white squares with a white pine in the middle represents the unity this document codified and was originally depicted on a belt made of wampum.

clues.clue: The centrally located Onondaga were granted the title "Keepers of the Fire" by the Great Law of Peace, hosting the ceremonial capital and its Grand Council
examples: Known as the Great Law of Peace, this document was the culmination of the work of the Peacemaker and granted the centrally-located Onondaga the title "Keepers of the Fire".
Representatives in this polity, symbolized by an eastern white pine, convened at an eternal fire preserved by tie-breaking "firekeeper" representatives from its capital constituency.

clues.clue: Some subgroups were designated "Keepers of the Western Door" (Seneca) and "Keepers of the Central Fire," alongside the Mohawk "Keepers of the Eastern Door"
examples: Some subgroups of these people were designated "Keepers of the Western Door" and "Keepers of the Central Fire".

clues.clue: Representatives had to publicly debate proposals to reach unanimity within their "moiety" before other constituencies could do the same
examples: Representatives in this polity had to publicly debate a proposal to unanimity with the others from their constituency before other groups in their "moiety" could do the same and pass it to the remaining "senior" or "junior moiety" constituencies.

clues.clue: Chiefs (sachems) could be demoted by clan mothers via "knocking off the horns"; deposed lords were stripped of the "deer's antlers," symbolically returned to the women of their heritage
examples: Deposed lords of these people would be stripped of the "deer's antlers", which would symbolically be returned to the women whose heritage they were.
This group's chiefs, called sachems, could be demoted by the women in the group in a decision called "knocking off the horns."

clues.clue: "Condolence Canes" were used to record succession among the Confederacy's rulers
examples: This polity used "Condolence Canes" to record succession amongst its rulers.

works.name: The Beaver Wars and Early Expansion
works.description: In the 17th-century Beaver Wars, the Confederacy fought France and its Huron allies to control the fur trade, killing several Jesuit missionaries in the process, displacing rival peoples, and eventually expanding out of upstate New York into the Ohio Country.

clues.clue: Fought France and Huron allies during the Beaver Wars, a conflict over the fur trade in which the Dutch sold the Confederacy guns
examples: This tribe fought the French and the Huron during the Beaver Wars, and its leader Joseph Brant allied part of it with the British during the American Revolution.
The Dutch sold guns to these people during a series of conflicts against the French-supported Huron called the Beaver Wars.

clues.clue: Jesuit missionaries Isaac Jogues, John de Brebeuf, Charles Garnier, Gabriel Lallemant, and Jean de Lalande were killed during the conflict; some are counted among the Eight North American Martyrs
examples: Working from a neighboring people's mission at Sainte-Marie, missionaries like Isaac Jogues and John de Brebeuf were killed by these people.
This tribe was partially founded by a man who was called the "Great Peacemaker." During a conflict, this tribe killed the Jesuit missionaries Charles Garnier and Gabriel Lallemant.

clues.clue: Fifty captured members were sent to Marseilles as galley slaves after being taken at Fort Frontenac
examples: 50 people of this group were sent to Marseilles as galley slaves after being captured at Fort Frontenac.

clues.clue: Was defeated by the French at the Battle of Sorel, after which Louis-Hector de Calliere negotiated a peace treaty
examples: Louis-Hector de Calliere negotiated one peace treaty with this polity, which had previously been defeated at the Battle of Sorel.

clues.clue: Early on, displaced the Quapaw, Mosopelea, and Tutelo peoples and defeated the Neutral Nation
examples: Early in their history, this people displaced the Quapaw, Mosopelea, and the Tutelo and also defeated the Neutral Nation.

clues.clue: Expanded into the Ohio Country from its base in upstate New York during the Beaver Wars, after trading furs to Europeans for firearms
examples: This confederacy, which at the time included the Onondaga, Oneida, and Mohawk, expanded into the Ohio Country out of their base in upstate New York during the Beaver Wars after trading furs to Europeans for modern weaponry.

clues.clue: Replaced fallen family members by adopting captives taken in raids called "Mourning Wars"
examples: Members of this group replaced fallen family members by adopting captives taken in Mourning Wars.

clues.clue: The Susquehannock, though speakers of an Iroquoian language, never joined the Confederacy and instead fought it during the Beaver Wars
examples: The Indian tribe in question is the Susquehannock, one of the few tribes that spoke a language of this group not to join its namesake confederacy. The Susquehannock fought this confederacy in the Beaver Wars.

works.name: Diplomacy with Europeans: the Covenant Chain and Treaties
works.description: The Confederacy negotiated a long series of treaties with European colonial powers, most notably the Covenant Chain alliance with the British, symbolized by the term "kaswentha" for coexistence, and sent delegations abroad even as later treaties like the 1744 Treaty of Lancaster and the Nanfan Treaty stripped away its territory.

clues.clue: Signed a series of agreements with British colonists called the Covenant Chain
examples: This polity signed a set of agreements called the Covenant Chain.
Name this confederation of Indigenous tribes that had earlier signed the Covenant Chain. Its Grand Council government included tribes like the Seneca and Mohawk.

clues.clue: "Kaswentha" refers to the relationship the Confederacy negotiated to coexist with foreign powers
examples: The term "kaswentha" refers to the relationship this polity negotiated to coexist with foreign powers.

clues.clue: A treaty metaphorically named for a link between a symbol of the Confederacy and a sailing ship included phrases like "as long as the sun shines upon the earth" and "as long as the waters flow"
examples: A treaty named metaphorically for a link between a symbol of these people and a sailing ship included phrases such as "as long as the sun shines upon the earth", "as long as the waters flow", and "as long as the grass grows green, peace will last".

clues.clue: Four "kings," including Hendrick Peters (erroneously called "Emperor"), went on an diplomatic mission to Great Britain, later painted as three of the "Four Kings of the New World" on an 1710 visit
examples: Hendrick Peters, erroneously referred to as "Emperor" of this polity, was among four "kings" from this polity who went on a diplomatic mission to Great Britain.
On a 1710 visit, leaders of these people were painted as three of the "Four Kings of the New World."

clues.clue: Ceded parts of its western territory in the Nanfan Treaty
examples: This polity, which ceded parts of its western territory in the Nanfan Treaty, previously agreed to the Covenant Chain with Great Britain.

clues.clue: A German-born diplomat (Conrad Weiser) earned the title "Holder of the Heavens" for negotiating peace treaties alongside James Logan
examples: A German-born diplomat in this polity earned the title "Holder of the Heavens" for negotiating peace treaties alongside James Logan.

clues.clue: The "half-king" Tanacharison conducted trade and diplomacy at Logstown before his arrest by Governor Duquesne
examples: A "half-king" in this polity named Tanacharison conducted trade and diplomacy at Logstown before his arrest by Governor Duquesne.

clues.clue: At the 1744 Treaty of Lancaster, the Confederacy ceded claims to land in Virginia and Maryland; negotiator Thomas Bladen helped the British swindle away this land
examples: Joseph Brant allied many of its members with Britain during the American Revolution, and they signed the Treaty of Lancaster in 1744, giving up claims to land in Virginia and Maryland.
In negotiating a 1744 treaty, Bladen helped the British swindle land away from the six nations of this confederacy.

works.name: The Iroquois Influence Thesis
works.description: A controversial historiographical claim, advanced in works like Donald Grinde and Bruce Johansen's Exemplar of Liberty and recognized by a 1988 U.S. Senate resolution, holds that the Confederacy's Great Law of Peace and a speech by the Onondaga leader Canassatego at the 1744 Treaty of Lancaster influenced the framers of the U.S. Constitution.

clues.clue: A 1988 U.S. Senate resolution recognized the Confederacy's Great Law of Peace for its influence on the ideals of the Constitution
examples: A 1988 Senate resolution recognized this confederacy's Great Law of Peace for its influence on the ideals of the Constitution. Hiawatha is credited as one of the founders of this confederacy.

clues.clue: Cadwallader Colden's early academic study of the Confederacy noted its "great men" were poorer than its "common people"; Donald Grinde and Bruce Johansen's Exemplar of Liberty later defended the controversial "influence thesis" about it
examples: The earliest academic study of this polity, by Cadwallader Colden, notes that its "great men" were poorer than its "common people." Donald Grinde and Bruce Johansen's Exemplar of Liberty defended the controversial "influence thesis" concerning this polity.

clues.clue: A leader's speech at the Treaty of Lancaster apocryphally inspired the plan to unify the colonies at the Albany Congress
examples: A speech given by a leader in this polity at the Treaty of Lancaster apocryphally inspired the plan to unify the colonies at the Albany Congress.

works.name: Longhouse Religion and Handsome Lake
works.description: In the early 19th century, the prophet Handsome Lake spread a Quaker-influenced revitalization movement among the Confederacy called Gai'wiio, or the Longhouse Religion, building on older beliefs and practices such as the False Face Society and the condolence ceremony.

clues.clue: The prophet/"Messiah" Handsome Lake spread the Quaker-influenced Gai'wiio, known as the Longhouse Religion, still practiced among the Seneca
examples: A revitalization movement among this people was spurred by the prophet Handsome Lake.

Another Native American who preached abstaining from alcohol was Handsome Lake, whose longhouse-based religion of Gai'wiio is still practiced by the Seneca, one of the six nations of this Native American confederacy.

clues.clue: The Longhouse Religion encoded teachings in a wampum language legendarily invented by Hiawatha
examples: what people's Longhouse Religion encoded teachings in the wampum language legendarily invented by Hiawatha?

clues.clue: J.N.B. Hewitt described belief in "otgun," a nefarious counterpart to the mana-like concept of "orenda"
examples: Hewitt described this people group's belief in "otgun," a nefarious counterpart to the mana-like concept of "orenda."

clues.clue: Arthur C. Parker translated the "Good Message," describing an "evil one" in a gold castle who sent abortion and witchcraft
examples: Parker translated the "Good Message" of a member of this people that tells of an "evil one" who lived in a gold castle and sent them such evils as abortion and witchcraft.

clues.clue: After deaths, held a condolence ceremony; healers may belong to the False Face Society, which wore red or black painted masks
examples: After deaths, these people held a condolence ceremony and raided their neighbors as part of their "mourning wars." These people, whose healers may belong to the False Face Society, include a Catherine who was the first canonized American Indian.
The False Face Society among this group wore red or black painted masks as they healed people.

clues.clue: Catherine Tekakwitha, associated with the mission at Kahnawake, became the first canonized American Indian saint
examples: The mission Kahnawake was a longtime home for one of these people baptized as Catherine, Tekakwitha.

clues.clue: One leader asked, having already "taken all the land," why white missionaries also needed to spread their religion, refusing proposed missionary activity
examples: A leader of these people asked why, having taken all the land, white missionaries needed to spread their religion in refuting proposed missionary activity.

works.name: The American Revolution: Joseph Brant and the Sullivan-Clinton Expedition
works.description: During the American Revolution, most of the Confederacy sided with the British under the Mohawk leader Joseph Brant, splitting loyalties and prompting massacres of colonists that led George Washington to order the devastating Sullivan-Clinton Expedition against Confederacy territory.

clues.clue: Mostly fought against the American Revolution (allied with the British) under Mohawk leader Joseph Brant
examples: These people fought against the American Revolution under Joseph Brant.
Sullivan's Expedition targeted these people after a series of raids by their leader, Joseph Brant.

clues.clue: Divided loyalties among member nations during the Revolution led to the Battle of Oriskany
examples: The Cherry Valley Massacre sparked the Continental Congress to order John Sullivan to attack the territory of some of these people, culminating in the Battle of Newtown, after divided loyalties among them led to the Battle of Oriskany.

clues.clue: The Cherry Valley Massacre, carried out by Confederacy warriors and Loyalists, sparked the Continental Congress to order John Sullivan to attack Confederacy territory, culminating in the Battle of Newtown
examples: The Cherry Valley Massacre sparked the Continental Congress to order John Sullivan to attack the territory of some of these people, culminating in the Battle of (*) Newtown.

clues.clue: Confederacy warriors allied with Loyalist forces (including Butler's Rangers) in the Wyoming Massacre, a "Six Nations" alliance including the Mohawk and Seneca
examples: The Indians who allied with Butler in the Wyoming Massacre belonged to this Indian confederacy of "Six Nations" such as the Mohawk and Seneca.

clues.clue: In response to the Cherry Valley and Wyoming Massacres, George Washington (called the "Town Destroyer") ordered the 1779 Sullivan-Clinton Expedition, a scorched-earth campaign that burned Confederacy towns and crops in the Finger Lakes region and broke its power in western New York; a survivor named Madam Sacho was encountered during the campaign
examples: Madam Sacho, who survived the Town Destroyer's attacks against this government, was encountered during the Sullivan Campaign.

During the American Revolution, the Americans responded to the Cherry Valley and Wyoming Massacres with this 1779 punitive expedition that burned the towns and crops of the Iroquois in the Finger Lakes region.

clues.clue: After the Revolution, members who had sided with the British gave up land won 150 years earlier in the Beaver Wars, relocating to the Haldimand Grant in Ontario under the 1784 Treaty of Fort Stanwix, represented by Joseph Brant
examples: Some of these people gave up land they'd won in the Beaver Wars 150 years earlier and relocated to the Haldimand Grant in Ontario under the terms of the 1784 Treaty of Fort Stanwix, where they were represented by Joseph Brant.

clues.clue: The rivalry between missionary Samuel Kirkland and a Confederacy leader (who tried to keep the group neutral) is documented in Alan Taylor's book The Divided Ground
examples: The rivalry between Samuel Kirkland and a leader of these people was documented in Alan Taylor's book The Divided Ground.

works.name: Aftermath: Ely Parker and 19th-Century Legal Cases
works.description: In the 19th century, the Seneca statesman Ely Parker became the Confederacy's most prominent public figure, drafting the Confederate surrender at Appomattox and later winning a major land-rights case against the Ogden Land Company.

clues.clue: Ely Parker, a member of the Confederacy, wrote the instrument of surrender at Appomattox Court House
examples: The surrender treaty at Appomattox Court House was written by one of these people, Ely Parker.
one member of this group, Ely Parker, wrote the instrument of surrender at Appomattox.

clues.clue: Ely Parker prevailed against the Ogden Land Company in the 1857 case Fellows v. Blacksmith, which held the Treaty of Buffalo Creek could only be used by the federal government to force the defendant to give up his sawmill
examples: Ely Parker, who was one of these people, prevailed against the Ogden Land Company in the 1857 case Fellows v. Blacksmith, which held that the Treaty of Buffalo Creek could only be used by the federal government to force the defendant to give up his sawmill.

works.name: Anthropological Study and Literary Depictions
works.description: The Confederacy became a foundational case study for American anthropology through Lewis Henry Morgan's fieldwork and Anthony Wallace's research, while it also appears fictionalized as recurring antagonists in James Fenimore Cooper's Leatherstocking Tales.

clues.clue: Lewis Henry Morgan performed extensive fieldwork on the Confederacy's customs, writing an ethnography of the group and naming his "bifurcate merging" kinship-system typology after them
examples: Morgan performed a tremendous amount of field research about the customs of this Native American tribe, whose "Great Law of Peace" may have influenced parts of the US constitution.
Lewis Henry Morgan wrote an ethnography of this group and named one of his types of kinship systems after them.

clues.clue: Anthony Wallace wrote the paper "Revitalization Movements" after fieldwork studying the Confederacy and its religion
examples: Wallace wrote the paper "Revitalization Movements" after fieldwork studying this people group and their religion.

clues.clue: In James Fenimore Cooper's Leatherstocking Tales, the Iroquois are depicted as "bad Indians" (led by the character Magua in The Last of the Mohicans) contrasted with the "good" Delaware; in The Deerslayer they capture Hurry Harry and Thomas Hutter
examples: In The Deerslayer, Hurry Harry and Thomas Hutter are captured by this Indian people. Throughout the Leatherstocking Tales, Cooper frequently contrasts "good Indians" like the Delaware with this group of "bad Indians," who are led by Magua in The Last of the Mohicans.

comprehensive_summary: The Iroquois Confederacy — also called the Haudenosaunee, the Five Nations, or (after the Tuscarora joined in the 18th century) the Six Nations — was a political alliance of Native American peoples based in upstate New York, uniting the Mohawk, Oneida, Onondaga, Cayuga, and Seneca. Its founding is credited to the semi-mythical "Great Peacemaker" (Deganawidah), who reportedly suffered a severe speech impediment and was assisted by Hiawatha and the "Mother of Nations" Jigonsaseh in instituting the Great Law of Peace; founding legend holds that Confederacy leaders uprooted a pine tree and buried their weapons in the hole, partly out of fear of the neighboring Algonquians. The Great Law of Peace (Gayanashagowa), an oral constitution recorded on wampum belts, established a Grand Council of representatives symbolized by an eastern white pine, permitted amendment through a process called "adding to the rafters," gave clan mothers power to confirm or deny the inheritance of political offices (and to depose chiefs, or "sachems," by "knocking off the horns," with a deposed lord's "deer's antlers" symbolically returned to his female relatives), allowed non-hereditary advisors likened to "newly-sprung pine trees," and granted the centrally located Onondaga the title "Keepers of the Fire" as the seat of an eternal council fire; other subgroups were called "Keepers of the Western Door" and "Keepers of the Central Fire," and succession was recorded using "Condolence Canes." In the 17th-century Beaver Wars, the Confederacy — armed with Dutch guns — fought France and its Huron allies for control of the fur trade, killing Jesuit missionaries including Isaac Jogues, John de Brebeuf, Charles Garnier, and Gabriel Lallemant (some later canonized as North American Martyrs), sending 50 captives to Marseilles as galley slaves after a defeat at Fort Frontenac, losing the Battle of Sorel to the French before Louis-Hector de Calliere negotiated peace, displacing the Quapaw, Mosopelea, and Tutelo, defeating the Neutral Nation, and expanding into the Ohio Country; the Susquehannock, despite speaking an Iroquoian language, never joined and instead fought the Confederacy in this conflict. The Confederacy negotiated extensively with European powers, signing the Covenant Chain series of treaties with the British (grounded in the coexistence principle of "kaswentha" and a treaty pledging peace "as long as the sun shines"), sending four "kings" (including Hendrick Peters, wrongly called "Emperor") on a diplomatic mission to Britain in 1710, ceding western territory in the Nanfan Treaty, and relying on diplomats like the "Holder of the Heavens" and the "half-king" Tanacharison (arrested by Governor Duquesne) before ceding land in Virginia and Maryland at the 1744 Treaty of Lancaster, aided by British negotiator Thomas Bladen. That treaty's Onondaga speaker, Canassatego, is central to the controversial "Iroquois influence thesis" — defended by Donald Grinde and Bruce Johansen's Exemplar of Liberty and recognized by a 1988 U.S. Senate resolution — that the Great Law of Peace shaped the U.S. Constitution; Cadwallader Colden's pioneering study of the Confederacy had earlier observed that its "great men" were poorer than its "common people." In the early 19th century the prophet Handsome Lake spread a Quaker-influenced revitalization movement called Gai'wiio, or the Longhouse Religion (still practiced by the Seneca), building on older beliefs like "otgun" (a nefarious counterpart to "orenda," per J.N.B. Hewitt), the "Good Message" of a gold-castle-dwelling "evil one" (per Arthur C. Parker), the condolence ceremony, and the mask-wearing False Face Society; Catherine Tekakwitha, tied to the Kahnawake mission, became the first canonized American Indian saint. During the American Revolution, most of the Confederacy allied with the British under Mohawk leader Joseph Brant, and divided loyalties produced the Battle of Oriskany; Confederacy-Loyalist raiders carried out the Wyoming Massacre and the Cherry Valley Massacre, prompting the Continental Congress to order John Sullivan's attack (climaxing at the Battle of Newtown) and then George Washington's 1779 Sullivan-Clinton Expedition, a scorched-earth campaign (during which a survivor named Madam Sacho was encountered) that devastated Confederacy towns in the Finger Lakes and broke its power in western New York; the missionary rivalry underlying these divided loyalties, between Samuel Kirkland and a Confederacy leader, is documented in Alan Taylor's The Divided Ground. Afterward, pro-British members relocated to the Haldimand Grant in Ontario under the 1784 Treaty of Fort Stanwix. In the 19th century, the Seneca statesman Ely Parker became the Confederacy's most prominent figure, drafting the Confederate surrender instrument at Appomattox Court House and later winning the 1857 case Fellows v. Blacksmith against the Ogden Land Company over the Treaty of Buffalo Creek. The Confederacy also became foundational to American anthropology through Lewis Henry Morgan's extensive fieldwork (which produced his "bifurcate merging" kinship typology) and Anthony Wallace's paper "Revitalization Movements," and appears fictionalized as recurring antagonists — led by the character Magua in The Last of the Mohicans, and capturing Hurry Harry and Thomas Hutter in The Deerslayer — in James Fenimore Cooper's Leatherstocking Tales.
